Lab Guide › Professional Development

Attending a conference

Eligibility, funding, choosing a conference, and the full approval-to-share-back workflow.

  • Start guide: Undergraduates
  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

  • Work expectations
  • Time off & vacation

  • Pay for things
  • Travel
  • Ship packages
  • Lab operations roles

Professional Development

  • Attending a conference
  • Applying for funding
  • Request recommendation letters
  • File your dissertation

Conferences are a great way to meet the experts in your field, learn the state of the art, and enjoy talking about research.

Eligibility

  • If you’re first author on a lab paper that year, the lab will typically fund one relevant conference per calendar year (subject to available funds).
  • Want to attend more than one? Apply for travel awards or student support — Christina will help.
  • Before you book anything, get PI approval for (a) the conference, (b) the estimated budget, and (c) what you’re presenting. Without advance approval, reimbursement is not guaranteed.
  • Two weeks before travel, present a practice talk or poster in lab meeting.
  • After, share five minutes of takeaways and update the lab publications list.

Funding

Typically lab-funded: registration, travel (flight / train / mileage), lodging, reasonable meals and local transit, and poster printing.

Not covered: extra nights for personal travel, upgrades (business class, premium hotels), alcohol or expensive meals, and avoidable late-booking fees.

How payment works: in most cases you pay upfront and get reimbursed — keep itemized receipts. If the lab needs to pay directly (e.g., registration on the P-card), request that during the approval step.

Choosing a conference

Pick conferences that maximize audience fit, feedback quality, and career value. It’s valuable to alternate between engineering- and biology-focused conferences over time, but choose what best serves your project and goals this year.

Conference Field Frequency Month
AIAA Aviation Engineering Annual June
AIAA SciTech Engineering Annual January
GRC Multifunctional Materials & Structures Engineering Biennial January
SEB Biology Annual Varies
SICB Biology Annual January

The workflow

  1. Plan and get approval (before submitting or booking). Send Christina the conference name, dates, and location; what you’re submitting (poster / talk / paper); a rough budget; and whether you’ll apply for travel awards (recommended). Don’t book flights or hotels until you get a written “yes.”
  2. Submit an abstract. Conference titles can be less declarative than journal titles, since results can evolve. The Nature summary-paragraph style is a good default structure.
  3. Write the conference paper (if required). Keep it tight; aim for a version that can grow into a journal submission after incorporating feedback.
  4. Practice your presentation to the lab at least two weeks before you travel, so you can incorporate feedback first.
  5. Attend and represent the lab well. Network intentionally and use good judgment in professional settings.
  6. Share back at the next lab meeting: five minutes of top insights and one or two actionable ideas, and update the publications list.

Logging time away. Record time out of office in the Slack Vacation Tracker with the conference name as the reason. Conference days don’t count against your vacation allowance unless you extend for personal time.
